name: Endowed Progress + Goal Gradient + Fresh Start
overview: Three complementary motivation theories about goal pursuit. (1) ENDOWED PROGRESS EFFECT (Nunes & Dreze, 2006): people complete goals faster when given an artificial head start; the 'pre-stamped' car wash card with 2/10 stamps already filled doubled redemption rate vs an 8-stamp blank card (34% vs 19%, ~1.79x). (2) GOAL GRADIENT HYPOTHESIS (Hull, 1932/1934): organisms accelerate effort as they near a reward — rats run faster in the final segments of a maze, and Kivetz/Urminsky/Zheng (2006) showed coffee drinkers buy faster as they approach a free coffee. (3) FRESH START EFFECT (Dai, Milkman & Riis, 2014): temporal landmarks (Mondays, month starts, birthdays, January 1st) demarcate mental accounting periods, dissociating users from past failures and triggering aspirational behavior — gym attendance jumps 33-47% after these landmarks. Together they form the architecture of progress-bar / streak-recovery / weekly-reset UI patterns.
originator_or_company: Endowed Progress: Joseph C. Nunes (USC Marshall) & Xavier Dreze (UCLA Anderson, deceased 2012), 2006 Journal of Consumer Research. Goal Gradient: Clark L. Hull (Yale, behaviorist), 1932 Psychological Review and 1934 Journal of Comparative Psychology; resurrected for consumer research by Ran Kivetz (Columbia), Oleg Urminsky (Chicago Booth), Yuhuang Zheng (Tsinghua), 2006 Journal of Marketing Research. Fresh Start Effect: Hengchen Dai (UCLA Anderson), Katherine L. Milkman (Wharton), Jason Riis (Wharton/NYU), 2014 Management Science. Popularization: Katy Milkman's 'How to Change' (2021); BJ Fogg, Wendy Wood (habit science adjacent).
key_resources: [The Endowed Progress Effect: How Artificial Advancement Increases Effort - Nunes & Dreze (2006), SSRN](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=991962)
[The Goal-Gradient Hypothesis Resurrected - Kivetz, Urminsky & Zheng (2006), full PDF](https://home.uchicago.edu/ourminsky/Goal-Gradient_Illusionary_Goal_Progress.pdf)
[Hull (1934) The Rats' Speed of Locomotion Gradient in the Approach to Food - APA PsycNet](https://psycnet.apa.org/doiLanding?doi=10.1037/h0072640)
[The Fresh Start Effect: Temporal Landmarks Motivate Aspirational Behavior - Dai, Milkman & Riis (2014), Management Science](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/mnsc.2014.1901)
[Fresh Start Effect Wharton PDF (Dai, Milkman, Riis 2014)](https://faculty.wharton.upenn.edu/wp-content/uploads/2014/06/Dai_Fresh_Start_2014_Mgmt_Sci.pdf)
[Put Your Imperfections Behind You - Dai, Milkman, Riis (PMC follow-up)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4839284/)
[Designing for motivation with the goal-gradient effect - Ian Batterbee, UX Collective](https://uxdesign.cc/designing-for-motivation-with-the-goal-gradient-effect-c873cdf58beb)
[Knowledge Cuts Both Ways: When Progress Bars Backfire - Irrational Labs](https://irrationallabs.com/blog/knowledge-cuts-both-ways-when-progress-bars-backfire/)

[Katherine Milkman's Fresh Start Study Becomes Perennial Media Favorite - Wharton Magazine](https://magazine.wharton.upenn.edu/digital/katherine-milkmans-fresh-start-study-becomes-perennial-media-favorite/)
core_mechanics: (A) ENDOWED PROGRESS — definition: motivation to complete a task increases when the user perceives that some of the task is already done, even when the head start is artificial. Nunes & Dreze field study: 300 customers received a stamp card at a Los Angeles car wash. Group A: 8 stamps required, blank card. Group B: 10 stamps required, 2 stamps pre-stamped (real work = 8 in both arms). Result: 19% (Group A) vs 34% (Group B) reached redemption — a ~1.79x lift. Mechanism: shifting reference point makes the user re-categorize themselves as 'already started,' which activates loss aversion (sunk-progress aversion) and goal commitment. Threshold: the 'free' progress must feel earned-adjacent (e.g., 'welcome bonus,' 'profile import') rather than arbitrarily granted; obvious manipulation collapses the effect. (B) GOAL GRADIENT — definition: behavior accelerates as the perceived distance to the goal shrinks. Hull (1934) measured rat locomotion via electrical contacts in a straight runway and found monotonic speed increase from start to food box. Kivetz/Urminsky/Zheng (2006) Study 1: 'buy-10-get-1-free' coffee cards distributed at a cafe; inter-purchase intervals shrank as customers approached free coffee — operationally, the time between purchase #9 and #10 was shorter than between #1 and #2. Study 2 (online song-rating reward program, n=10,795): same shrinkage pattern. Study 3 (illusory progress): subjects given a 12-stamp card with 2 free 'bonus' stamps completed the 10 required purchases faster than subjects on a regular 10-stamp card — median completion 12.7 vs 15.6 days (significant). Combined goal-gradient + endowed-progress: post-reward, customers reduced engagement, then re-accelerated as second reward neared. Customer retention insight: those who accelerated more strongly toward reward 1 were more likely to re-engage. (C) FRESH START EFFECT — definition: temporal landmarks (start of week/month/year/semester, birthdays, holidays, life events like moves and graduations) trigger goal-pursuit behavior because they create a new mental accounting period that psychologically distances users from their pre-landmark imperfect self. Three archival studies: (1) Google search for 'diet' is +14.4% on January 1, +3.7% on the first day of a month, and elevated on Mondays (+10-25% over midweek), with January searches +120% over mid-year. (2) University gym attendance (Wharton Penn dataset, 1+ year): +33.4% on Mondays vs midweek, +47.1% at semester start, +25% on month start, large spike at New Year. (3) Pursuit of online commitment-device contracts (stickK.com): elevated after temporal landmarks. Mechanism: 'temporal disassociation' — users compartmentalize past failure into a closed mental account, see themselves as a 'new' decision-maker. Boundary condition: the landmark must signal a meaningful new beginning; meaningless landmarks (e.g., random Tuesdays) do not work, and landmarks that emphasize continuity with the past (negative meaning) can suppress the effect. UI INTEGRATION: progress bar at 0% feels insurmountable — endowed progress justifies starting at 10-30%; goal gradient justifies subdividing long flows into bands so each segment offers a near-term completion sprint; fresh start justifies weekly-reset rituals (Monday morning re-onboard, monthly digest, birthday push) and post-failure 'clean slate' UX after streak loss.
designer_or_researcher_voice: Joseph C. Nunes & Xavier Dreze (2006): 'Consumers presented with a reward program requiring eight purchases, who were given two preliminary stamps as an artificial advancement toward the goal, completed the ten required purchases faster than consumers required to make eight purchases starting from scratch... The results suggest that perceived progress toward a goal energizes goal-directed behavior.' (Journal of Consumer Research)

Ran Kivetz (Columbia GSB), on goal gradient resurrected: 'A rational economic actor should treat each coffee purchase identically; what we found instead is that consumers behave like Hull's rats — they accelerate as the goal nears, and they accelerate even more when given an illusion of progress.' (Columbia Business School research summary, 2006)

Katherine Milkman (Wharton): 'Temporal landmarks like the start of a week, month, or year cause people to feel disconnected from their past selves, including past failures. That psychological gap motivates aspirational behavior — but only briefly. The window closes within a few weeks.' (paraphrased from How to Change, 2021, and Wharton Magazine interview)

Hengchen Dai (UCLA Anderson): 'We found 33.4% more gym visits on Mondays, even controlling for membership tenure. The Monday effect is not a behavioral artifact — it is a robust temporal-landmark effect.' (Dai, Milkman & Riis 2014, Management Science)

Jon Yablonski (Laws of UX): 'The Goal Gradient Effect describes how motivation to reach a goal increases as one gets closer. Apply this by providing artificial progress toward a goal to help motivate users to complete a task.' (lawsofux.com)

Kristen Berman / Irrational Labs: 'Progress bars cut both ways. They motivate when progress is perceived as feasible, but they demotivate when users see how far they have to go and feel the goal is unreachable. Calibration matters more than the bar itself.' (Irrational Labs blog, paraphrased)
metrics_or_evidence: ENDOWED PROGRESS — Nunes & Dreze (2006) car wash field experiment: 34% redemption vs 19%, ~1.79x lift, persisted across 9-month observation window (n=300). LinkedIn profile completion progress meter (Reid Hoffman / Jeff Weiner era): widely-cited internal claim of +55% profile-completion rate from never-zero starting state, though specific public source for this number is secondary (industry blogs, not LinkedIn IR). GOAL GRADIENT — Hull (1934): monotonic speed gradient measured via runway contacts, n=10 rats, replicated across 5+ subsequent behaviorist studies. Kivetz/Urminsky/Zheng (2006): coffee study n=~150 cards over ~6 months; mean inter-purchase time decreased ~20% from early to late stamps; illusory-progress study median completion 12.7 days (12-stamp w/ 2 bonus) vs 15.6 days (10-stamp regular), p<.05; song-rating study n=10,795, same pattern with effect surviving controls for individual heterogeneity. FRESH START — Dai/Milkman/Riis (2014) three archival studies: Google Trends 'diet' +14.4% on Jan 1, +3.7% first-of-month; Wharton/Penn gym attendance +33.4% Monday vs midweek, +47.1% semester start, +25% month start; stickK.com commitment-contract creation elevated post-landmark. Follow-up Dai et al. (2015, PMC): emphasizing user's birthday triggers similar landmark effect. Cross-domain: gamified onboarding flows commonly report 30-55% completion-rate lift from 'pre-filled' progress bar designs (Userpilot, Appcues case studies — observational, not RCT). Sailer & Homner (2020) gamification meta-analysis (not specific to these three but adjacent): g=0.49 cognitive, g=0.36 motivational, g=0.25 behavioral. [uncertain] — exact LinkedIn 55% number is secondary-sourced; effect-size meta-analyses specifically pooling endowed-progress and goal-gradient field studies are not published.
criticisms: (1) ENDOWED PROGRESS — fragile: Brooke Tully and other replicators report the effect can collapse if the head-start is perceived as arbitrary or if the user is told the rules transparently (Coglode meta-summary). Conditional on the user accepting the head start as legitimate. (2) GOAL GRADIENT — rationality re-interpretation: economists (e.g., Stigler-style) argue accelerated late-stage purchases may reflect declining marginal cost of switching, not goal-gradient motivation; Kivetz et al. controlled for this but the controversy persists. (3) PROGRESS BARS BACKFIRE — Irrational Labs and HBR have shown that progress bars can demotivate users who realize how far they have to go, especially if the bar fills slowly in the first 20% (the 'mountain of effort' frame). (4) FRESH START — Dai, Milkman & Riis themselves and Beshears et al. (2021) found that fresh-start framing can BACKFIRE for high-performers: a baseball-trade study showed a 5% performance DECREASE for above-average players post-trade (vs +3.8% for below-average), suggesting fresh starts hurt people who were already on a roll. (5) FRESH START WINDOW — empirically narrow: most fresh-start motivation fades within 4-8 weeks (gym studies show February attrition cliff). (6) OVERJUSTIFICATION — when endowed progress is paired with extrinsic rewards, Self-Determination Theory predicts crowding-out of intrinsic motivation; gamification literature (Hanus & Fox 2015) confirms this for grade-incentive contexts. (7) DECEPTION RISK — artificial progress crosses into dark-pattern territory if users feel duped (Coglode and UX Collective both flag this). (8) MEANS-ENDS REVERSAL — users may complete trivial tasks just to fill the progress bar, not for the underlying value (parallel to streak gaming).
failure_cases: (1) MICROSOFT WINDOWS PROGRESS BARS (legacy): the inaccurate, randomly-jumping progress bar is a textbook bad-implementation case — when users learn the bar is unreliable, all three effects collapse. (2) DROPBOX 'GET STARTED' CHECKLIST early version: too many steps, slow first-step completion led to abandonment (later redesigned with 5 short steps and obvious quick wins). (3) FRESH-START NEW YEAR'S RESOLUTION CLIFF: ~80% of January gym joiners drop by mid-February ('Quitter's Day' is the second Friday of January per Strava data). (4) OVER-PROMISING ENDOWED PROGRESS: SaaS onboarding flows that show '50% complete!' when the user has done almost nothing trigger reactance — Userpilot and Appcues case studies cite multiple instances of users churning faster on flows perceived as patronizing. (5) FOURSQUARE BADGES (early 2010s): goal-gradient via badges initially boosted check-ins but failed to retain users beyond novelty effect; the company pivoted away from gamification ~2014. (6) HIGH-PERFORMER FRESH-START DAMAGE: Beshears et al. (2021) MLB trade study — top hitters performed 5% worse post-trade, the disruption from a fresh start undermined existing momentum. (7) GROUPON / RETAIL COUNTDOWN TIMERS: aggressive goal-gradient framing ('only 2 hours left!') has been flagged by EU DSA enforcement as manipulative when paired with fake urgency.
antipatterns: (1) FAKE / DECEPTIVE HEAD START — claiming '50% complete' when the user did nothing meaningful; backfires once the user notices and erodes trust. Confused with hostile dark patterns by Center for Humane Technology. (2) BACKLOADED PROGRESS BAR — bar that fills 80% in the first half of effort and crawls in the second half; produces frustration cliff. (3) UNCALIBRATED GOAL-GRADIENT URGENCY ('only 2 left!' fake scarcity) — flagged by EU DSA as manipulative. (4) FRESH-START GUILT — emphasizing 'you missed last week' alongside a Monday reset; the temporal landmark works only when it dissociates from past failure, not when it amplifies it. (5) ENDOWED PROGRESS WITHOUT MEANING — pre-filled checklist where the items are trivial busywork; means-ends reversal kicks in. (6) NEVER-COMPLETING PROGRESS BAR (LinkedIn 'profile strength' for years could not reach 100% even with full data) — destroys the close-the-loop reward and turns the gradient into perpetual nagging. (7) FRESH-START SPAM — sending 'New Month, New You!' push every single month dilutes the landmark to noise. (8) PROGRESS BAR ON UNCONTROLLABLE EVENTS (e.g., loading times you cannot predict) — better to use indeterminate spinners; fake deterministic bars violate Apple HIG progress-indicator guidance.
applicability_to_shikaq: Strong fit for shikaq's 20s-female-cafe-iPad persona, but only with the QUIET / Apple-Fitness-aligned interpretation. Concrete translations: (a) ENDOWED PROGRESS in onboarding — when a user picks a target certification (IT/基本/SPI/簿記2), pre-fill the 'Today' Activity Ring at ~15% with a free 'welcome' contribution that surfaces as 'スタートボーナス' in the Sidebar — never claim it as the user's effort, frame it as 'shikaq からの応援'. Reduces Day-1 abandon. (b) GOAL GRADIENT in lesson structure — divide each course into Apple Books-style 'shelves' of 5-7 lessons (rather than monolithic 30-lesson courses); each shelf has a near-term completion ring so the gradient activates within a single session. Apple Podcasts-style episode-card progress indicators. (c) GOAL GRADIENT in mock exam — show 'あと3問' (remaining count) only in the final third of the test, not throughout, to avoid early-stage demotivation; final stretch gets a subtle Liquid-Glass tint ramp. (d) FRESH START — Monday morning 'New Week' Liquid-Glass card on Today screen with calm aspirational copy ('今週は10問だけ。それで十分です'); month-start version tied to a tiny Apple-Fitness-style trophy reset. Ship birthday landmark via profiles.birthday with consent. (e) POST-STREAK-LOSS RECOVERY — when a streak breaks, surface a 'Fresh Start Monday' CTA the next Monday (NOT immediately) — this is the most evidence-backed application of fresh-start research and pairs perfectly with the watchOS-11 'Pause Rings' philosophy from the Streak_Design research. (f) Avoid SPI/簿記-specific gamification of trivial mastery (means-ends reversal); anchor progress on questions correct, not lessons opened. (g) HEXAD coverage: Achiever (goal-gradient mock-exam ring), Free Spirit (custom weekly reset day instead of forced Monday — autonomy), Philanthropist (none required — these mechanics are individual). Avoid Player/Disruptor patterns. Aesthetic: SF Symbols 'flag.checkered.2.crossed' for goal markers, 'sparkles' for fresh-start moment, semantic systemBlue tint for ring, semantic resource colors per cert (itPassport / fe / spi / boki). NEVER cartoon countdown, NEVER red urgency banners.
phase_assignment: Phase 1 (3 weeks): endowed-progress on Today screen (welcome-bonus pre-filled ring at ~15% on first-cert-selection) — highest activation lift, smallest implementation. Phase 2 (4-6 weeks): goal-gradient lesson chunking (Apple Books shelves, Apple Podcasts cards with embedded progress) + Monday Fresh Start card on Today screen. Phase 3 (6-8 weeks): birthday/month-start landmark notifications (calm copy, opt-in), post-streak-loss Fresh-Start-Monday recovery flow, Apple Fitness-style monthly trophy reset. Rationale: Phase 1 because endowed progress requires only one onboarding tweak with strong evidence-base; Phase 2 because lesson chunking touches DB schema (sections/lessons hierarchy already exists per db-schema.md); Phase 3 because birthday/landmark scheduling needs notification infrastructure from Streak_Design Phase 2.
integration_points: Sidebar: subtle 'スタートボーナス' badge on first launch only (auto-dismisses after 7 days). TodayContent: ActivityRings/GoalRing component receives a pre_filled_pct prop (default 0, set to 0.15 on first-cert-selection); Monday card via existing TodayContent layout slot. RightDetail: agenda surfaces 'Fresh Start Monday' or 'New Month' headers when applicable. Practice / mock exam screens: progress-bar component with band-based Liquid-Glass tinting in final third. DB additions: profiles.welcome_bonus_applied_at timestamp, profiles.birthday date (nullable, opt-in), profiles.fresh_start_day_of_week int default 1 (Monday). New table: temporal_landmarks (user_id, type 'monday'/'month_start'/'birthday'/'cert_anniversary', triggered_at, completed bool) for analytics. Edge Function: weekly cron (timezone-aware) generates fresh_start cards; nightly cron checks birthday/month-start. Lessons schema: sections already provide chunking; add sections.lesson_count_target int for goal-gradient micro-goals. Notifications: reuse Streak_Design APNs/FCM stack with new categories ('fresh_start_monday', 'fresh_start_month', 'birthday_landmark', 'streak_recovery_monday'). RLS: own-data on temporal_landmarks; admin-bypass for support. Frontend: new component <FreshStartCard /> + <EndowedProgressRing /> sharing the same SVG primitive as ActivityRings/GoalRing.
risks: (1) DECEPTION PERCEPTION — if the welcome-bonus ring fill is too aggressive (e.g., 50%) or unexplained, 20s-female cafe-iPad users who are media-literate will feel patronized; cap at 10-20% and label transparently as 'shikaq からのスタートボーナス'. (2) FRESH-START NOTIFICATION FATIGUE — sending Monday + month-start + birthday all at once dilutes each landmark; rate-limit to maximum 1 landmark notification per week. (3) FRESH-START BACKFIRE FOR HIGH-PERFORMERS — Beshears et al. show fresh-start framing hurts users on a roll; suppress fresh-start CTAs for users currently in an active streak (>14 days) — only surface to lapsed/at-risk segments. (4) PROGRESS-BAR DEMOTIVATION — if the goal-gradient bar shows too long a journey, users abandon; use Apple Books shelf chunking (5-7 lessons per shelf) so each near-term goal feels reachable in one cafe session. (5) MEANS-ENDS REVERSAL on lesson completion — users may speed-click through lessons just to fill the bar; anchor progress on quiz_results correct-answers, not lessons opened (consistent with Streak_Design mastery-anchored streak). (6) BIRTHDAY DATA PRIVACY — Japan APPI requires explicit opt-in for birthday collection; gate behind a clear consent toggle, never auto-derived. (7) APPLE APP REVIEW — pre-filled progress bars are scrutinized post-2025 'manipulative engagement patterns' guidance; transparent 'welcome bonus' framing keeps within bounds. (8) METRICS BIAS — endowed-progress headline metric (Day-1 onboarding completion) is easy to game; supplement with Day-30 retention and questions-correct to ensure real learning lift, not just activation theater.
apple_hig_compatibility: compatible with adaptation. Apple HIG endorses progress indicators (UIProgressView, indeterminate vs deterministic) and the Activity Rings model embodies a calibrated goal-gradient design. Endowed progress crosses into 'manipulative' territory only if the head start is hidden or deceptive — transparent 'welcome bonus' framing is HIG-clean. Fresh-start cards align beautifully with Apple News+ Featured-card style (per design-principles.md mapping) and the Apple Fitness monthly-trophy reset. SF Symbols mapping: 'flag.checkered.2.crossed' (goal marker), 'sparkles' (fresh-start moment), 'sunrise.fill' (Monday morning), 'gift.fill' (welcome bonus). Liquid Glass card on Today screen for Monday/month-start landmark. NEVER use red urgency, NEVER use cartoon countdown timers, NEVER backload the progress bar — all three violate Clarity/Deference. Material Symbols Outlined (web side) provides equivalents.
ethical_score: neutral with positive-leaning execution. Center for Humane Technology framework: progress mechanics are not inherently harmful (unlike streaks-with-loss-aversion-stacks), but become harmful when (a) deception is used to manufacture progress, (b) fresh-start landmarks are weaponized as guilt triggers ('you missed last week — start over!'), (c) goal-gradient urgency is paired with fake scarcity. shikaq's recommended execution (transparent welcome bonus, calm Monday cards, mastery-anchored progress, no urgency banners) lands net-positive. Risk to time-regret is LOW because these mechanics encourage starting and resuming, not endless engagement; risk to dependency is LOW because they reset and renew rather than escalating; risk to burnout is LOW because fresh-start logic explicitly creates rest-and-restart rhythms.
evidence_quality: rigorous_RCT (endowed progress + goal gradient + fresh start all have published peer-reviewed field experiments and archival studies in top journals — Journal of Consumer Research, Journal of Marketing Research, Management Science). Hull's original goal gradient: rigorous behaviorist experiments (small n by modern standards but methodologically clean). Modern UX-application metrics (LinkedIn 55%, onboarding-flow 30-55% lifts) are observational/company case study and weaker. The three core academic findings are among the most-replicated in behavioral economics.
localization_fit_japan: high. Japanese culture aligns strongly with all three mechanics: (1) ENDOWED PROGRESS — スタンプカード (stamp cards at coffee shops, ramen shops, beauty salons) is a deeply embedded daily-life pattern; ポイントカード culture has trained Japanese consumers since the 1980s. The pre-stamped welcome bonus reads as 'お得感' / hospitality, not deception. (2) GOAL GRADIENT — 受験勉強 / カウントダウン / 模試の残り問題数 is a culturally familiar progress-tracking idiom; 'あと○問' framing is native. (3) FRESH START — 初日 / 月初 / 新年度 / お正月 / 誕生日 / 新学期 all carry strong cultural meaning, with 元日・節分・お盆 functioning as collective fresh-start landmarks. The Japanese 'kiri' / '区切り' concept (a clean break between time periods) is exactly the temporal-disassociation mechanism Dai/Milkman/Riis describe. The 20s-female career-switcher persona is specifically the demographic that buys 手帳 (planners) and posts 'New Month, New Goals' on Instagram — Fresh Start Monday cards land naturally. Caveat: avoid loud Western New Year framing (1月1日 is meaningful but Japanese new-year energy peaks on 元旦, not Dec-31 countdown); calibrate landmarks to Japanese calendar including 4月 (new fiscal/school year, especially salient for career-switchers preparing for 簿記2 / IT資格).
